concerns.text: FR-003 specifies the model formula `trust ~ politeness + conversation_length + (1|user_id) + (1|dialogue_id)`. In the PersonaChat and EmpatheticDialogues datasets, 'dialogue_id' is the unique identifier for the entire conversation. Including `(1|dialogue_id)` as a random effect when the outcome 'trust' is a single scalar rating per dialogue creates a singular design matrix (perfect collinearity). The random effect variance for dialogue_id will be zero or the model will fail to converge because the fixed effect of the dialogue (the trust rating itself) is being modeled against the dialogue's own identifier. This is a category error in mixed-effects modeling for this data structure.

The validation target 'trust_rating' is described as a '1-7 Likert' scale. Linear Mixed-Effects (LME) models assume a continuous, normally distributed outcome. Treating a discrete 7-point ordinal scale as continuous violates the distributional assumptions of the LME model (FR-003). The spec should mandate an Ordinal Mixed-Effects model (e.g., cumulative link mixed model) or justify the continuous approximation with a robustness check, as the current design risks biased standard errors and p-values.

The assumption that 'Persona-Chat and EmpatheticDialogues datasets contain trust/quality ratings' is factually incorrect for the standard releases of these datasets. These datasets typically contain 'quality' or 'naturalness' ratings (often 1-5) or no explicit 'trust' metric. 'Trust' is a specific psychological construct distinct from 'quality'. Using a proxy (quality) for the target variable (trust) without explicit validation of the proxy's psychometric validity (Constitution Principle VI) renders the hypothesis test invalid. The spec must either identify a dataset with explicit trust ratings or redefine the outcome to match the available data (e.g., 'perceived quality').

FR-005 requires comparing a 'lexicon-based classifier' against the primary 'open-source classifier' (implied to be a model like Politeness-BERT). If the lexicon-based classifier is trained or derived from the same theoretical framework as the primary model, or if the 'lexicon' is simply a subset of the features used by the primary model, the comparison is not independent. The spec must explicitly define the lexicon-based method (e.g., 'LIWC politeness dictionary') to ensure it represents a distinct methodological approach, otherwise the robustness check is trivial.
